title: Does Evangelion have a Five Act Story Structure
display_title: Does Evangelion have a Five Act Story Structure
wikitext:
[]: Does Evangelion have a Five-Act Story Structure?             Quote Post by The Abhorrent » Mon May 23, 2011 12:27 am Not too sure if this has been discussed before, but I did a brief check/search and didn't come across anything. As such, we have the topic question(s): Does Evangelion have a five act story structure? If not, what sort of story structure is it? How does this story structure affect the series? For referrence, here is the wikipedia article on the five-act (or dramatic) structure. The five acts are... 1. Introduction & Exposition -- Introducing the characters, establishing the world and the antagonist(s). 2. Rising Action -- The basic conflict following the intro. 3. Climax -- The turning point of the story, where the event after which things get better or worse for the characters. 4. Falling Action -- Where events are unravelling, which can be marked by suspense and doubt. 5. Denouement & Conclusion -- Fairly self-explanatory, the ending. Many of you may have likely heard of the three-act structure , and it is very similar to the five-act structure. My understanding is that the (currently more popular?) three-act is an evolution of the more traditional five-act, condensing the final three steps into a single one (climax & denouement). So then, how exactly does this affect Evangelion ? Perhaps the most obvious clue is that the ending can be called rather subdued, most stories in modern times end very shortly after the climax (following the three-act structure)... whereas Evangelion 's climax arguably occurs quite a bit before everything is said and done. Here's my quick analysis as to which parts of the series where each act takes place: Act I -- Episodes 1-6 The first six episodes of the series serve to establish the setting of the story and the characters. Shinji is introduced and his personal issues (the crippling lack of self-esteem), Rei's inability to express emotion, and the contrast between Misato's very unprofessional conduct at home and how well she does when everything is on the line. The Angels are introduced as a very credible threat, with Ramiel in particular requiring an elaborate plan to simply go toe-to-toe with. Act II -- Episodes 7-15 Episode 7 is a bit of a floater, it belong to either of the first two acts; however, it's generally more upbeat the first six and is being lumped in with the second act. Episode 8 is very notable for being Asuka's introduction, and is the point where the events kick into high gear. The second act is by far the most action-packed, with the protagonists defeating an Angel in each of the episodes from 8 to 13; it is the "Monster of the Week" arc of the series (you can technically include episode 7 into the MotW segment, a giant rampaging robot is a close enough fit I think). Episode 14 is a bit of a recap, and episode 15 focuses on the interactions between the characters... but they don't quite fit into the next act and are included here as a result. Act III -- Episodes 16-19 The climax is the turning point of the story, and in these four episodes things change quite a bit . Episode 17 is the only one without an encounter with an Angel, but is largely to set up the encounter with one in the next episode. How each of these three Angels are slain bear a very strong similarity: Eva-01 went berserk and ripped them all to shreds, each time ending the battle covered in blood and roaring in triumph. Okay, maybe not that last part for the 12th & 13 Angels... but Eva-01's berserker state is shown three times in relatively quick succession and played for horror each time. The third time is particularly jarring because Eva-01 demonstrates that it is very much alive (and considering the two Angels preceeding it.... how did we not see that one coming?). Act IV -- Episode 20-24 These episodes are marked by two key elements: the mysteries of the series being revealed and the characters' descent into despair. Episode 20 is a bit of a transition phase and may be better suited for Act III, but whatever. Episode 21 finally shows the backstory of the series (including Misato's backstory and her being at the Second Impact), and serves to break Misato by killing off Kaji. Episode 22 reveals Asuka's backstory and how her mother was driven to insanity (Kyoko's suicide isn't shown until the start of episode 24), and how she was broken is fairly obvious. Episode 23 features the death of Rei (II) and we are shown part of the reason she is so mysterious. Shinji is progressively being driven to despair through these three episodes, by having the events force him away from each of the three central female characters; episode 24 serves to be the last straw for him, with Kaworu being the final Angel and the only friend Shinji had when he needed it but still has to kill in order to save humanity. Act V -- Third Impact The lead up to 3I in The End of Evangelion could arguably be lumped into Act IV, but it could also be called the transition between these acts just as easily. Nevertheless, Third Impact is the definite denouement and conclusion to Evangelion; be it either episodes 25 & 26 or the visual spectacle seen in EoE. All the characters are forced to confront their personal demons as a result of 3I, and Shinji makes his ultimate decision. The term "resolution" was avoided for reason here, as Evangelion deliberately ends in a situation that just begs the question "What happens next?" ... but it's still definitely the ending to series. Is my analysis correct? How else did it affect the series?

[]: Re: Does Evangelion have a Five-Act Story Structure?             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon May 23, 2011 1:27 am The Abhorrent wrote: Is my analysis correct?

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Mon May 23, 2011 2:05 am Eva doesn't really follow any this kind of clear structure. Anno really just made it up all along as he went on, without much forethought in terms of structure. The shift in mood of series happens in ep 14 which was in production at the same time ep 16 was, so in terms of actual production 14/16 was the turning point. But by no means it's the climax I have hard time identifying Eva's climax anyway. Ep 24 or Third Impact would fit, I guess

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Mon May 23, 2011 9:09 am Actually, I've already posted a topic like that some time ago. They totally reinvented the falling Action, even if it was because of their own depriness. I doubt it was intentionally like this, since, uh, why should it be? and also what Xard said earlier, but it HAS a five act structure - they probably independently ended up with the same structure Aristotle made up some millenia ago because it's practical/has certain adventages, just like desert plants everywhere on the world end up looking cactus-like.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon May 23, 2011 9:42 am For a more detailed reply, I think it's better to view the series as a series of short arcs, often with a breakbeat episode between them to clean our mental palates. 1-3: So who/what is this show about? 4: This is NOT just a giant robot show 5-6: What's the deal with Rei? 7: Misato and a bit of world-building 8-12: Asuka and the many joys of teamwork 13: Ritsuko and the Magi 14: Recap and subtle setups for every plotline of the rest of the series 15: Dysfunctional people have dysfunctional relationships 16-20: Piloting stops being fun and Shinji's motivations are explored. 21: Fleshing out the adults and history of nerv 22-23: Asuka and Rei are revealed for who they really are 24: The final trial 25-26: What was it all for? or 25'-26': Just kidding, we aren't done making your life hell yet.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Mon May 23, 2011 10:37 am I say these five greater storyarcs do exist (maybe it's just coincidence that they're five) Each has their little climax (act I: Operation Yashima, Act II: Sahaquiel, Act III: Zeruel, Act IV: Kaworu's Death, Act V is the finale anyways/the rejection of TI) towards the end and marks a different step in the character's mostly parabolic developement with each having their disting feeling and status quo. I think it's fair to call 14 to 20 the "peak" because this is where the characters reach thei heights of their positive developement before stuff goes down the drain/choices made here affect the downfall. Also, there are some episodes that ate rather"on the fence" which is a) a sighn that the structure was not intentionally meant as mirroring a classical drama à la aristotle, but more of a logical consequence of the parabolic or S-line ish developement line of the characters. and b) only realistic since ppl don't change from one day to another. These are: a) Episode 7. Stuff from the next arc (EVA 02) is already mentioned, we have a monster-of-the-week, Shinji is already closer to his action arc personality (Capable of complaining and talking back to ppl instead of curling up into a ball right away. He's still a mild person, but the difference is very aparent) and takes place after ep 6's big showdown, but I'd still add it to the first act because it is still part of the Shinji learning to find/establishing himself a home - storyline. He is more worried about someone else than his own fear in a battle, makes the first positive experiences there ("I caught up! *little smile*), the transformation is not yet complete/is completed here, we see him still "get used" to life with Misato, he regognizes her as a family Member in the end, bonding with his friends happens etc... I would NOT add ep 8 to the first act because while it instrudes a main character, it adds Asuka into an already established world - that's what Asuka is, a foreighn element, a new thing that throwns Shinji's life into dissaray, the "confusing female sex" teenage boys someday become aware of. b) Episode 13 - follows the Action Arc structure with a monster of the week and days-in.the-limelight, but takes place after it's great climax. The children don't do too much. Duh, I'd add it to the action arc. c) episiode 14: It has recaps of stuff that happened before, but the point of it is having the seele ppl debate it... The "deep questions" already start being asked, they already start talking of the Dummy Plug (important plot point of the third act that may as well be called "Dummy Plug Arc" ) and this beginns to shift Rei back into focus. I'd add it to the third act. sam,e goes for ep 15 which has very important peak- and turningpoints for many characters, mainly the Kaji/Misato, the Shinji/Asuka and the Shinji/Gendo relationship. Asuka's descent already somewhat beginns here. d) episode 20 - It is very much like the fourth-act episodes atmosphere- and style wise (Instrospection, ppl debating their dark situations, that sense of waiting for destiny to strike again..." But sort of concludes the plot from ep 19 and has the climax (no pun intended) of Kaji's and Misato's relationship. I'd still act it to the third one.

[]:             Quote Post by The Abhorrent » Mon May 23, 2011 10:47 am NemZ wrote: Nope. well, of course you can MAKE it fit the 5-act structure (or the 3-act, whatever), but the story isn't really build that way... and in any case 19 is NOT the real climax because Shinji's moment of badassitude doesn't actually change anything at all.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Mon May 23, 2011 10:56 am If you want to talk about the narrative structure of Eva, perhaps it is better to think it from the Japanese perspective - which is much more familiar with a 4-act concept ( Kishoutenketsu ) than a 5-act concept used in the west.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Mon May 23, 2011 10:58 am If anything, episodes 14-16 are the beginning of the Rising Action of the story. It's at this point that things start to become more serious, the character drama really starts to develop, and things become more and more fucked up. The Climax is really hard to identify, but I'd say for sure that it's during 25-26/25'-26'. This is where things reach their breaking point, and Shinji is forced to confront himself and really decide to make a real change in order to survive. This is appropriate, because Evangelion lacks a conclusion, for better in my opinion. EoTV ends on the "Congratulations" note, but we're never really told what it means for certain; this ambiguity has lead people to believe that this is actually a BAD END/Instrumentality Conclusion. As for EoE, it ends with the return to Earth and One More Final ; we're told that Shinji is making a positive change, but that scene on the shore shows us otherwise; and then we don't know what happens. We can't tell if anything has been resolved, and if it has we have very little idea of where its going.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Mon May 23, 2011 11:08 am @Sachi: That'd bee "three act logic". EVA is about the only more or less modern story with something like "falling action" that I know today... Which doesn't mean stuff becomming more boring or slower no, no, that's when the tragedies just keep unfolding, so the suspense sort of falls (the mysteries are lifted, you can tell it's going downhill... ) While EoE/EoTV are clearly the finale, it consists mostly of downfall, tough both have a hopeful conclusion at the end, that could also be anticipated from ppl previous choises (Shinji always came back...) 14 to 20 is where everything is possible, where the direction changes, in particular the bardiel incident/Zeruel battle.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Mon May 23, 2011 11:15 am symbv wrote: If you want to talk about the narrative structure of Eva, perhaps it is better to think it from the Japanese perspective - which is much more familiar with a 4-act concept ( Kishoutenketsu ) than a 5-act concept used in the west.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Mon May 23, 2011 11:20 am ^ @Xard I never paid much attention to the structure of Eva tv series, but if we want to touch on the number of acts I would turn immediately to a 4-act structure which is often taught in writing classes in Japan and is common in modern Japanese literature. The more classic Japanese literature and music has the Jo-Ha-Kyu 3-act structure, though I suspect in Rebuild it is still more the modern 4-act structure (there are 4 movies) but they want to make it look classic so they adopted the Jo-Ha-Kyu naming convention.

[]:             Quote Post by The Abhorrent » Mon May 23, 2011 12:10 pm symbv wrote: If you want to talk about the narrative structure of Eva, perhaps it is better to think it from the Japanese perspective - which is much more familiar with a 4-act concept ( Kishoutenketsu ) than a 5-act concept used in the west.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Mon May 23, 2011 12:37 pm They made four parts of it for Rebuild and ppl weren't all that happy about 8 to 12 and 15 to 19 being squeezed together. I say all the important stuff was in there, but it did take some restructuring/squeezing together. I don't think they thought much about storyarcs arcs (Some stuff mentions part A and part B, with the rift in ep 14), but ended up with a 5-ish structure because of the parabolic/S-line developement of the characters.

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Mon May 23, 2011 1:21 pm Kendrix wrote: They made four parts of it for Rebuild and ppl weren't all that happy about 8 to 12 and 15 to 19 being squeezed together. I say all the important stuff was in there, but it did take some restructuring/squeezing together.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Mon May 23, 2011 7:47 pm Ages ago, we split the show into four parts for the drop-down menu on the old commentary page. I never put much thought into why -- maybe it was mostly Tines' doing? -- but I honestly can't find fault with it. It seems like the most natural possible fit. Prologue: 01-06 Action: 07-13 Descent: 14-19 End: 20-26

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Mon May 23, 2011 7:57 pm Except stuff doesn't start going downhill from 14 onwards - different things reach their "peaks" during 14 to 20... Tough I'd have to agree, 21 to 24 and 25 to 26 may be the most suitable ones to be squeezed together atmosphere wise - Still, the despair event horrizont is distinctly crossed at the end of ep 24... or maybe it just feels like there's a line because there's where the endings diverge? Or because ep 24 has a hope spot? Because the "big finale " should be separated? Dunno. Maybe there's no "line" at all. Anyway, I'm glad that Rebuild chose to compress 8 to 12, because... well, I miss stuff,(I lovelovelovelove Episode 11) but I wouldn't have wanted them to compress the last part.

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Mon May 23, 2011 8:01 pm I think if we want to do this correctly, we want to look at the subplots, and see how they affect the situation. After that, we determine the acts for the subplots. After, we take those, and compare them to what at least some agree would be the acts of the main plot, deciding whether or not the subplot affects the main plot enough to determine whether an act begins, continues, or ends. We also have to keep in mind that: 1. An act does not have to begin, or end at the end of an episode. 2. The acts do not have to be of equal length (for example, EOE could be the entire series's end of climax and conclusion).

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Mon May 23, 2011 8:01 pm Kendrix wrote: Except stuff doesn't start going downhill from 14 onwards - different things reach their "peaks" during 14 to 20..

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Mon May 23, 2011 8:06 pm Well, seen by "overarching plot-thingies" 1 to 7 (That's why I add 7) is "Shinji establishing a home for himself/becomming a fighther", 8 to 13 is "The characters and teamwork", 14 to 20 is "The Dummy Plug and its consequences" (Also puts Rei back into the mix and starts asking em big questions) 25/26 are the big finale and everything in between is zeh ordeal/stuff falling apart.../introspection/motive analysis ahoy.

Article: Does Evangelion have a Five Act Story Structure
[]: Does Evangelion have a Five-Act Story Structure?             Quote Post by The Abhorrent » Mon May 23, 2011 12:27 am Not too sure if this has been discussed before, but I did a brief check/search and didn't come across anything. As such, we have the topic question(s): Does Evangelion have a five act story structure? If not, what sort of story structure is it? How does this story structure affect the series? For referrence, here is the wikipedia article on the five-act (or dramatic) structure. The five acts are... 1. Introduction & Exposition -- Introducing the characters, establishing the world and the antagonist(s). 2. Rising Action -- The basic conflict following the intro. 3. Climax -- The turning point of the story, where the event after which things get better or worse for the characters. 4. Falling Action -- Where events are unravelling, which can be marked by suspense and doubt. 5. Denouement & Conclusion -- Fairly self-explanatory, the ending. Many of you may have likely heard of the three-act structure , and it is very similar to the five-act structure. My understanding is that the (currently more popular?) three-act is an evolution of the more traditional five-act, condensing the final three steps into a single one (climax & denouement). So then, how exactly does this affect Evangelion ? Perhaps the most obvious clue is that the ending can be called rather subdued, most stories in modern times end very shortly after the climax (following the three-act structure)... whereas Evangelion 's climax arguably occurs quite a bit before everything is said and done. Here's my quick analysis as to which parts of the series where each act takes place: Act I -- Episodes 1-6 The first six episodes of the series serve to establish the setting of the story and the characters. Shinji is introduced and his personal issues (the crippling lack of self-esteem), Rei's inability to express emotion, and the contrast between Misato's very unprofessional conduct at home and how well she does when everything is on the line. The Angels are introduced as a very credible threat, with Ramiel in particular requiring an elaborate plan to simply go toe-to-toe with. Act II -- Episodes 7-15 Episode 7 is a bit of a floater, it belong to either of the first two acts; however

Article: Does Evangelion have a Five Act Story Structure
, with Ramiel in particular requiring an elaborate plan to simply go toe-to-toe with. Act II -- Episodes 7-15 Episode 7 is a bit of a floater, it belong to either of the first two acts; however, it's generally more upbeat the first six and is being lumped in with the second act. Episode 8 is very notable for being Asuka's introduction, and is the point where the events kick into high gear. The second act is by far the most action-packed, with the protagonists defeating an Angel in each of the episodes from 8 to 13; it is the "Monster of the Week" arc of the series (you can technically include episode 7 into the MotW segment, a giant rampaging robot is a close enough fit I think). Episode 14 is a bit of a recap, and episode 15 focuses on the interactions between the characters... but they don't quite fit into the next act and are included here as a result. Act III -- Episodes 16-19 The climax is the turning point of the story, and in these four episodes things change quite a bit . Episode 17 is the only one without an encounter with an Angel, but is largely to set up the encounter with one in the next episode. How each of these three Angels are slain bear a very strong similarity: Eva-01 went berserk and ripped them all to shreds, each time ending the battle covered in blood and roaring in triumph. Okay, maybe not that last part for the 12th & 13 Angels... but Eva-01's berserker state is shown three times in relatively quick succession and played for horror each time. The third time is particularly jarring because Eva-01 demonstrates that it is very much alive (and considering the two Angels preceeding it.... how did we not see that one coming?). Act IV -- Episode 20-24 These episodes are marked by two key elements: the mysteries of the series being revealed and the characters' descent into despair. Episode 20 is a bit of a transition phase and may be better suited for Act III, but whatever. Episode 21 finally shows the backstory of the series (including Misato's backstory and her being at the Second Impact), and serves to break Misato by killing off Kaji. Episode 22 reveals Asuka's backstory and how her mother was driven to insanity (Kyoko's suicide isn't shown until the start of episode 24), and how she was broken is fairly obvious

 to break Misato by killing off Kaji. Episode 22 reveals Asuka's backstory and how her mother was driven to insanity (Kyoko's suicide isn't shown until the start of episode 24), and how she was broken is fairly obvious. Episode 23 features the death of Rei (II) and we are shown part of the reason she is so mysterious. Shinji is progressively being driven to despair through these three episodes, by having the events force him away from each of the three central female characters; episode 24 serves to be the last straw for him, with Kaworu being the final Angel and the only friend Shinji had when he needed it but still has to kill in order to save humanity. Act V -- Third Impact The lead up to 3I in The End of Evangelion could arguably be lumped into Act IV, but it could also be called the transition between these acts just as easily. Nevertheless, Third Impact is the definite denouement and conclusion to Evangelion; be it either episodes 25 & 26 or the visual spectacle seen in EoE. All the characters are forced to confront their personal demons as a result of 3I, and Shinji makes his ultimate decision. The term "resolution" was avoided for reason here, as Evangelion deliberately ends in a situation that just begs the question "What happens next?" ... but it's still definitely the ending to series. Is my analysis correct? How else did it affect the series?

16 are the beginning of the Rising Action of the story. It's at this point that things start to become more serious, the character drama really starts to develop, and things become more and more fucked up. The Climax is really hard to identify, but I'd say for sure that it's during 25-26/25'-26'. This is where things reach their breaking point, and Shinji is forced to confront himself and really decide to make a real change in order to survive. This is appropriate, because Evangelion lacks a conclusion, for better in my opinion. EoTV ends on the "Congratulations" note, but we're never really told what it means for certain; this ambiguity has lead people to believe that this is actually a BAD END/Instrumentality Conclusion. As for EoE, it ends with the return to Earth and One More Final ; we're told that Shinji is making a positive change, but that scene on the shore shows us otherwise; and then we don't know what happens. We can't tell if anything has been resolved, and if it has we have very little idea of where its going.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Mon May 23, 2011 11:08 am @Sachi: That'd bee "three act logic". EVA is about the only more or less modern story with something like "falling action" that I know today... Which doesn't mean stuff becomming more boring or slower no, no, that's when the tragedies just keep unfolding, so the suspense sort of falls (the mysteries are lifted, you can tell it's going downhill... ) While EoE/EoTV are clearly the finale, it consists mostly of downfall, tough both have a hopeful conclusion at the end, that could also be anticipated from ppl previous choises (Shinji always came back...) 14 to 20 is where everything is possible, where the direction changes, in particular the bardiel incident/Zeruel battle.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Mon May 23, 2011 11:20 am ^ @Xard I never paid much attention to the structure of Eva tv series, but if we want to touch on the number of acts I would turn immediately to a 4-act structure which is often taught in writing classes in Japan and is common in modern Japanese literature. []:             Quote Post by Stryker » Mon May 23, 2011 8:01 pm I think if we want to do this correctly, we want to look at the subplots, and see how they affect the situation. After that, we determine the acts for the subplots. After, we take those, and compare them to what at least some agree would be the acts of the main plot, deciding whether or not the subplot affects the main plot enough to determine whether an act begins, continues, or ends. We also have to keep in mind that: 1. An act does not have to begin, or end at the end of an episode. 2. The acts do not have to be of equal length (for example, EOE could be the entire series's end of climax and conclusion).
